coredata.dc:title: Multi-emission fluorescent sensor array based on carbon dots and lanthanide for detection of heavy metal ions under stepwise prediction strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zijun
Chen, Jiao
Liu, Yuying
Wang, Xiyuan
Shi, Qingdong
coredata.dc:description: Pollution by heavy metals represents a serious threat to both the environment and human health. A facile multi-emission fluorescence sensor array based on carbon dots (QR-CDs) and a novel lanthanide complex (EDTA-Tb3+) was constructed, and is capable of obtaining simultaneous, multidimensional data, which can improve the detection efficiency and accuracy when it comes to multiple heavy metal ions. To meet the challenges of establishing a unified model, we built an innovative unified model (SX-model) by the “stepwise prediction” strategy combined with machine learning methods to obtain optimal screening methods. This model integrates classification and concentration models under the framework of the tree-based pipeline optimization technique (TPOT). Then, the extreme random forest (ERF) was selected as the classification model method with the highest accuracy among various methods through TPOT. This sensor array demonstrated sensitive detection of seven heavy metal ions in the range of 0.05–50 μM with an accuracy of 95.6%. The ability to identify binary mixed samples simultaneously and effectively was greatly enhanced. Furthermore, the metal ions in 288 real samples (obtained from lake water and soil samples) were effectively identified with 93.3% and 100% accuracy, respectively. The proposed original SX-model-assisted multi-emission sensor not only overcomes issues regarding low sensibility but also breaks the bottleneck of analysis methods, showing great application potential in the array detection field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lanthanide
Multi-emission sensor array
Heavy metal ions
Stepwise prediction strategy

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of QR-CDs and lanthanide complexes
Detection of heavy metal ions with the QR-CDs / EDTA-Tb3+ sensor array
Characterization of QR-CDs and lanthanide complexes (EDTA-Tb3+)
Differentiation of binary mixtures with SX-model processing
Distinguishing metal ions in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (Inset: HRTEM images of QR-CDs); (b) the size distribution of the QR-CDs; (c) XPS spectrum of the QR-CDs; (d) FT-IR spectrum of the QR-CDs; (e) the fluorescence spectra of QR-CDs; (f) the fluorescence spectra of EDTA-Tb3+.

Flow diagram of model construction. The black arrow represents the SX-model construction process, the red arrow represents the identification process of test sample. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Identification of the heavy metal ions using the SX-model in deionized water (a) the heat map of QR-CDs / EDTA-Tb3+ with heavy metal ions (10 μM); (b) the fluorescent responses of the heavy metal ions based on (F0-F)/F0 (10 μM); (c) the classified prediction confusion matrix heat map; (d) the linear fitting curve representing relationship between the true category and predicted category.

Identification of binary mixtures using the SX-model in deionized water (a) the heat map of QR-CDs / EDTA-Tb3+ with binary mixtures (10 μM, 6:4); (b) the eigenvalue importance; (c) the classified prediction confusion matrix heat map (d) the linear fitting curve representing relationship between the true category and predicted category.

Identification of the heavy metal ions and binary mixtures using the SX-model in soil (a) the classified prediction confusion matrix heat map with heavy metal ions; (b) the classified prediction confusion matrix heat map with binary mixtures.
Schematic representation of multi-emission array sensor for the establishment and detection of heavy metal ions.

head.ce:title: Multi-emission fluorescent sensor array based on carbon dots and lanthanide for detection of heavy metal ions under stepwise prediction strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Ecology and Environment, Xin Jiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment Sciences, Xin Jiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment Sciences, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources and Environment Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment Sciences, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Ecology and Environment, Xin Jiang University, Shengli Road, Tianshan District, Urumqi 830046, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources and Environment Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pollution by heavy metals represents a serious threat to both the environment and human health. A facile multi-emission fluorescence sensor array based on carbon dots (QR-CDs) and a novel lanthanide complex (EDTA-Tb3+) was constructed, and is capable of obtaining simultaneous, multidimensional data, which can improve the detection efficiency and accuracy when it comes to multiple heavy metal ions. To meet the challenges of establishing a unified model, we built an innovative unified model (SX-model) by the “stepwise prediction” strategy combined with machine learning methods to obtain optimal screening methods. This model integrates classification and concentration models under the framework of the tree-based pipeline optimization technique (TPOT). Then, the extreme random forest (ERF) was selected as the classification model method with the highest accuracy among various methods through TPOT. This sensor array demonstrated sensitive detection of seven heavy metal ions in the range of 0.05–50 μM with an accuracy of 95.6%. The ability to identify binary mixed samples simultaneously and effectively was greatly enhanced. Furthermore, the metal ions in 288 real samples (obtained from lake water and soil samples) were effectively identified with 93.3% and 100% accuracy, respectively. The proposed original SX-model-assisted multi-emission sensor not only overcomes issues regarding low sensibility but also breaks the bottleneck of analysis methods, showing great application potential in the array detection field.

ce:keyword.ce:text: inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution can be a serious threat to the environment and human health[1]. Heavy metal ions can enter the human body through the food chain or through direct contact and accumulate in the body. This causes lesions in the nervous, blood, and urinary systems and the most severe cases can even result in deaths[2–4]. To date, there are many methods for the sensitive detection of heavy metal ions, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS)[5], inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES)[6], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS)[7], and fluorescent chemosensors[8]. The fluorescent sensing strategy prevalent as it is a simple, inexpensive, and sensitive method, which enables real-time online detection[9–11]. However, owing to the complexity of substances found in the environment, traditional fluorescence sensors may face challenges in detecting a variety of heavy metal ions. To meet these challenges, fluorescent sensor array detection has attracted attention as an alternative method, owing to its advantages of having increased variety, simplicity, high accuracy, and high selectivity, as well as being more time-efficient[12].

A sensor array is a biomimetic molecular recognition method built by simulating an animal olfactory or gustatory system[13,14]. In most fluorescent sensor arrays, each sensor unit has a single-channel signal output[15]. In such systems, the signal of each sensor unit needs to be collected individually through array separation, which requires repeated measurements. Therefore, the multiple emission sensors can be used to avoid separating the spatial array of sensing units. Moreover, the multi-emission sensor can be self-calibrated to eliminate fluctuations in fluorescence intensity[16–18] and obtain multidimensional data simultaneously, providing more accurate results in practical applications. Notably, Wang et al. established a multiple emission sensor array, based on upconversion nanoparticles, to detect antioxidants[19].

Choosing the proper fluorescent materials as sensor elements is the key to building sensors for pollutant detection. Carbon dots (CDs) are attractive fluorescent indicators owing to qualities such as widely-sourced raw materials, strong and stable fluorescence, good water solubility, and good biocompatibility[20–22]. In recent years, lanthanide ion-based luminescent materials have been extensively studied because of their unique optical properties such as narrow luminescent band, long luminescent life, high color purity, and large Stokes shift[23–25]. In general, it is difficult to produce fluorescence by direct energy excitation because the lanthanide ions (Ln3+) f-f transitions are blocked. Thus, antenna ligands containing chromophores bind to Ln3+ to produce lanthanide complexes that sensitize lanthanide luminescence via energy transfer (antenna effect)[26,27]. At present, many ligands such as nucleotides and proteins, can be sensitized to the luminescence of Ln3+ by chelating with Ln3+. Owing to its characteristic sensitization process, Ln3+ has been widely used for fluorescence sensing[28,29]. Han et al. used guanosine monophosphate and deoxy-guanosine monophosphate coupled with Tb3+, respectively, to discriminate multiple metal ions[30]. Xue et al. built a sensor array based on DNA coupled with Tb3+, to monitor metal ions[31].

Machine learning (ML) is an important tool that allows computers to learn from and make predictions on data. Some studies have improved the accuracy of machine learning predictions by adding incremental algorithms, spatial information and changing inter-class distances[32–34]. At present, principal component analysis (PCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), support vector machine (SVM), and artificial neural networks (ANNs) are commonly used to analyze the data in array detection[35–38]. Wang’s group developed a fluorescent sensor array consisting of three different nitrogen-doped CDs, LDA was used to build a recognition model for each concentration gradient (100, 50, 20, and 10 μM) used to determine six metal ions[39]. However, appropriate machine learning and analysis methods can effectively improve the detection accuracy. Owing to various discrepant analysis methods of array sensing detection, there is an urgent demand for the development of an optimal model, based on multiple methods. Furthermore, the currently reported model has been built separately at each concentration and lacks a unified model. Therefore, array detection cannot be applied in real environments owing to the unavailability of an appropriate model to process and analyze the response data of unknown samples. Moreover, these models lack generality and are difficult to apply to a wider range of research. It is necessary to build a unified model to quickly screen the optimal analysis and data processing, which can be used to correctly identify unknown samples.

In this study, we selected fluorescence sensor array based on carbon dots (QR-CDs) and a novel lanthanide complex (EDTA-Tb3+) to design a multi-emission fluorescence sensor array, among seven heavy metal ions, including Cr6+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+, and Ni2+ (Scheme 1 ). QR-CDs were synthesized using quinaldine red (QR) as the raw material. EDTA as an antenna ligand sensitizes the fluorescence of the rare earth element Tb3+ (antenna effect). Owing to the good response between the metal ions and the multi-emission sensor, the fluorescence intensity (FI) changes as metal ions are added to the system. Based on the FI in the presence and absence of metal ions (F and F0), a fluorescence analysis pattern was created for each metal ion. Then, the “stepwise prediction” strategy, combined with machine learning was introduced for the first time, and the SX-model was established by integrating the classification model and the concentration model under the framework of the TPOT. Additionally, the optimal model was selected through TPOT, and the multidimensional data obtained by the array were further analyzed and processed using the SX-model. The results show that the seven heavy metal ions, at different concentrations, were successfully separated and differentiated in binary mixtures. This demonstrated the multi-emission fluorescence sensor array mode for simple, rapid, and low detection limit discrimination of multiple heavy metal ions. Overall, compared to the previous array analysis methods, this method offers a powerful solution by establishing a unified model that breaks the bottleneck of screening optimal methods.

Quinaldine red (QR), ethanediamine, dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), Tb (NO3)3, CrO3, Cu (NO3)2, FeCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, and FeCl3 were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). All reagents purchased from commercial suppliers were analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Deionized water was supplied by Xinjiang University (Urumqi, China). A dialysis bag (MWCO 2000 Da) was purchased from Biotopped Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan) was used to acquire all fluorescence spectra, and a UV–VIS spectrophotometer (U-3900H, Hitachi, Japan) was used to record the UV spectra. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired with an iS10 Thermo Nicolet spectrometer (USA), using the KBr pellet sample preparation method. By using an advanced data acquisition and processing system, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Escalab 250Xi, USA) was also used to maximize the amount of information extracted from experimental data. The morphology and microstructure of the QR-CDs were obtained through high-resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM; FEI Talos F200 series, 200 kV). For comparison of the prepared sensor, ICP-MS (Nex ION 300D) was used to measure the concentration of heavy metals in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of QR-CDs and lanthanide complexes
ce:section.ce:para: QR-CDs were synthesized according to a previously reported method with slight modifications[40]. In brief, 150 μL of ethanediamine were added to a QR aqueous solution (2 mM, 10 mL) and subsequently sonicated for 2 h at 50 °C. The reaction was terminated by adjusting the pH to neutral (7.0) with HCl. The QR-CDs solution was purified with CH2Cl2. Next, QR-CDs solutions were dialyzed against deionized water using a dialysis bag (MWCO membrane, 2000 Da) for 48 h to remove interfering substances.

For the lanthanide complexes (EDTA-Tb3+), fluorescence sensing probes were constructed by sensitizing the fluorescence of Tb3+ with EDTA. Different concentrations of EDTA were added to a Tb3+ aqueous solution (5 mM, 1 mL). The mixture was then incubated for 6 min at room temperature. Next, the fluorescence spectra of EDTA-Tb3+ were recorded after excitation at 284 nm. The optimal concentration of EDTA was also investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of heavy metal ions with the QR-CDs / EDTA-Tb3+ sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: QR-CDs/EDTA-Tb3+ were prepared and utilized as sensing elements to fabricate multi-emission sensor arrays. Aqueous solutions of 6 μM EDTA and 5 mM Tb3+ were mixed and incubated for 6 min, as the lanthanide complexes (EDTA-Tb3+). Then 0.5 μg mL−1 of a QR-CDs aqueous solution was added as two reporters to construct the QR-CDs / EDTA-Tb3+ sensor array for the detection of heavy metal ions.

Seven heavy metal ions (Cr6+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+, and Ni2+) at concentrations of 50 and 500 nM, and 1, 10, 20, 30, and 50 μM, were added to the sensor solution, containing QR-CDs (10 μL, 50 μg mL−1), EDTA (60 μL,100 mM), and Tb3+ (20 μL, 250 mM). The total volume of each sample was maintained at 1.0 mL. In addition, the heavy metal ions in the binary mixtures were analyzed. Fe2+/Cr6+, Cu2+/Cr6+ and Fe2+/Fe3+ were selected to prepare binary mixtures with molar ratios of 0:10, 4:6, 6:4, and 10:0, with the total concentration of the two heavy metal ions kept constant at 10 μM.

After incubation, the emission spectra of the mixtures were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence intensities at 400, 489 and 545 nm were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 284 nm. The fluorescence response value used was the difference in FI between the signal readings in the presence or absence of heavy metal ions. The fluorescence spectrum presents multiple peaks, including the front, middle, and rear peaks, which have intensities marked as F1, F2, and F3, respectively. F and F0 represent the FI in the presence and absence of heavy metal ions, respectively, and (F0-F)/F0 × 100% is used to represent the fluorescence response value and SX-model input data. To verify the predictive ability and reliability of the sensor array, nine replicates were measured for each experimental condition. Hence, a data matrix (3 × 7 metal ions × 9 replicates) was developed, which was further analyzed using Python version 3.6.

The classification accuracy was calculated using the formula: A/A0 × 100%, where A represents the correct sample quantity of classification prediction, and A0 represents the total sample quantity. The concentration accuracy was calculated using the formula: B/B0 × 100%, where B represents the sample concentration prediction value, and B0 represents the sample concentration true value.

Lake water and soil were selected as representative environmental samples, and were acquired from Ulungur Lake (Xinjiang, China). The water samples were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, after which a 0.22 µm membrane was used to filter the supernatant to exclude insoluble impurities[41]. In addition, the soil samples were pretreated as follows: In brief, 100 mL of deionized water was added to 50 g of the soil samples, which were then thoroughly stirred and mixed and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was first filtered with filter paper, and then was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble impurities. The treated water and soil samples were diluted 10 times by using deionized water. Four metal ions (Fe2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, and Fe3+) at concentrations of 500 nM, and 1, 5, and 10 μM were added to the real samples. The total volume of each sample was maintained at 1.0 mL. Using an excitation wavelength of 284 nm, the fluorescence spectra produced by the reaction with a well-mixed sample were recorded. For every concentration of the four metal ions, nine replicates were tested in parallel.

The array sensor detection data were processed and analyzed using a machine learning method with Python 3.6. The ERF combines the down-sampling majority class technique and the ensemble learning idea, and classes are represented in each tree by changing the class distribution. The training set was randomly selected from the original sample set T, and the allowance function is obtained as follows[42]:.
mg (X, Y) = avk I(hk (X) = Y) − max j
≠
k av k I(hk (X) = j).
where mg (X, Y) represents the difference between the average number of correct and false classifications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of QR-CDs and lanthanide complexes (EDTA-Tb3+)
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, QR-CDs and lanthanide complexes (EDTA-Tb3+) were used to construct a multichannel fluorescence sensor array to detect the seven heavy metal ions. The morphology, optical, and structural properties of the QR-CDs were characterized by TEM, UV absorption, fluorescence spectroscopy, XPS, and FT-IR. QR-CDs exhibit spherical structures, high mono-dispersity and relatively uniform distribution with mean diameter of 2.18 nm, with an overall range of 1.2–3.6 nm (Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b). In addition, clear crystal lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be observed in the HRTEM image of QR-CDs (Fig. 1a). Full-scan XPS spectra were employed to explore the chemical compositions and bonds of the QR-CDs. Specifically, the three peaks at approximately 285, 402, and 532 eV, related to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, can be identified (Fig. 1c). This further confirmed the presence of C, N, and O in the QR-CDS nanostructures[43,44]. As depicted in Fig. 1d, the FT-IR spectrum of QR-CDs revealed absorption peaks of stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H at 3468.65 cm−1, suggesting adequate water solubility. In addition, the peaks at 1637.89 cm−1 corresponded to C = C structures on the surface of QR-CDs[40,45].

Moreover, QR-CDs were colorless solutions in daylight, and emitted strong blue fluorescence under irradiation with 312 nm UV light. The results show that the maximum excitation and emission peaks of QR-CDs were at 320 nm and 400 nm (Fig. 1e), respectively. The quantum yield (QY) of QR-CDs was 13% using quinine sulfate (QY 54% in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4) as the reference.

The fluorescence spectra of Tb3+ and EDTA-Tb3+ were tested in aqueous solutions. The EDTA-Tb3+ results show the maximum excitation peak at 284 nm, and the maximum emission peaks at 489 nm and 545 nm (Figure S1). Fig. 1f shows that Tb3+ changes from weak fluorescence to significantly enhanced fluorescence with the addition of EDTA, when excited at 284 nm. EDTA-Tb3+ produced emission peaks at 489 and 545 nm, indicating that EDTA can sensitize Tb3+ to fluorescence (I.e. the antenna effect).

The optimum EDTA concentration for effectively sensitizing the fluorescence of Tb3+ was investigated. Figure S2 shows the fluorescence responses of EDTA-Tb3+ when different concentrations of EDTA were introduced into the Tb3+ aqueous solution. With increasing EDTA concentration, the emission peaks of Tb3+ at 489 and 545 nm gradually increased as well, reaching a maximum quality for sensitizing the fluorescence of Tb3+ at and EDTA concentration of 6 μM. Therefore, EDTA at 6 μM was selected for the following experiments. Additionally, it was found that the fluorescence of EDTA/Tb3+ was relatively stable after 6 min (Figure S3). Thus, a reaction time of 6 min for EDTA-Tb3+ was chosen for subsequent experiments.

To be suitable for practical sensing applications, QR-CDs and EDTA-Tb3+ should exhibit good fluorescence stability. Therefore, we investigated the influence of varying pH on fluorescence stability. At pH 7.0, the FI of QR-CDs is maximized (Figure S4). With increasing pH, the FI of EDTA-Tb3+ at 545 nm continuously increases (Figure S5). The following experiments were performed at pH 7.0 which optimizes the FI responses of both QR-CDs and EDTA-Tb3+, to different pH.

To construct the multichannel fluorescent sensor array, QR-CDs and lanthanide complexes (EDTA-Tb3+) were used as two independent sensor elements. The fluorescence response spectra of the QR-CDs/EDTA-Tb3+ complex system containing seven heavy metal ions (Cr6+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+, and Ni2+) were obtained at different concentrations. Our study investigated the FI variation ((F0-F)/F0) to establish an array detection platform (Fig. 3a, S6-S11).

EDTA can interact with metal ions through its carboxyl and amino groups. Therefore, it is reasonable to consider that metal ions binding to EDTA may affect the interaction of Tb3+ with EDTA, influencing the responses of EDTA-Tb3+. As illustrated in Figure S12, the absorption spectrum of the EDTA -Tb3+ changed with the addition of the heavy metal ions. Then the fluorescence lifetime of EDTA -Tb3+ was measured. As displayed in Fig S13, the fluorescence lifetime of EDTA -Tb3+ is 1160.5 μs, which is markedly longer than that of Tb3+ (426.2 μs). The EDTA -Tb3+ sensor tested with heavy metal ions showed a decline in fluorescence lifetime. These results showed that metal ions compete with Tb3+ to bind EDTA, leading to a weakened antenna effect and fluorescence quenching of EDTA-Tb3+.[30,46]. In addition, the presence of heavy metal ions in QR-CDs can alter their absorption, indicating the formation of a stable complex to generate static quenching (SQ) (Figure S14)[47,48].

Current data analysis methods for array detection face two main challenges: finding the optimal model from various machine learning methods, and a lack of a generalized model to enhance its practical applications. Therefore, we attempted to solve the above problems by building an SX model composed of two parts: a classification model and a concentration model, as shown in Fig. 2 . The value of (F0-F)/F0 × 100% is used to SX-model FI data, which F and F0 represent the FI in the presence and absence of heavy metal ions, respectively. Firstly, the classification model is uniquely established based on a FI data set without concentration labels. This allows unknown samples to be brought directly into the classification model and therefore the array detection can be applied in practice. The concentration model was established using a FI data set as a module to depict the logical relationship between samples in this study. A regression model between the concentration of each metal ion and the measured FI data was established. The built classification and concentration models were combined to construct the SX-model. Finally, the classification and concentration models used the TPOT pipeline to select the optimal machine learning method from various options available, these include SVM, Random Feature Attention (RFA), ERF and gradient boosted decision tree. The problem of selecting the optimal model from the various machine learning methods was thoroughly investigated. Based on the TPOT pipeline, the SX-model was no longer a fixed model. The SX-model could be automatically optimized according to the input data and has the potential to be extended for increased array detection. The identification process of the test samples in the SX-model is as follows:.

When the test FI data set was input into the SX-model, they passed the classification model to obtain the classification label, and then entered into the concentration model with the classification label. According to the classification label obtained in the previous step, the data set were substituted into the concentration model of the corresponding category. Thus, the classification and concentration information of the test samples were exported to identify the test samples. Through screening by TPOT, ERF was selected as the data processing method in the classification model, Ada boost regressor, K-neighbors regressor and Decision tree regressor adopted as the data processing method in the concentration model in this study. The parameters of model are listed in Table S1.

Multidimensional data were obtained through a multi-emission sensor array for the identification and quantification of heavy metal ions. The analytical performance of the seven single metal ions in deionized water was first investigated by qualitative analysis of seven concentrations (0.05–50 μM) (Fig. 3 a, Fig. 3b and Figure S6-S11). A total of 441 multidimensional data were used to build the SX-model, as shown in Fig. 3c. To test the ability of our sensor to distinguish unknown samples, 45 unknown samples were analyzed, and the classification accuracy was 95.6% (Table S2), displaying a linear relationship between the true category and predicted category, with an R2 of 0.73 (Fig. 3d). Additionally, the concentration accuracy for the unknown samples was found to be 100% (Table S3). The above results show that this model can be successfully used for the quantification of single metal ions in deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of binary mixtures with SX-model processing
ce:section.ce:para: As our sensor array has been successfully utilized to detect seven single metal ions, we further applied the sensor array for simultaneous detection of different binary mixture samples to deal with the complexity in environmental samples.

In the following work, binary mixtures (Fe2+/Cr6+, Cu2+/Cr6+ and Fe2+/Fe3+) mixtures were prepared with molar ratios of 0:10, 4:6, 6:4, and 10:0, respectively, in deionized water (Fig. 4 a and Figure S15). It is evident from the order of eigenvalue importance that the 400 nm data was the most important factor for enhancing the detection accuracy (Fig. 4b). As indicated in Fig. 4c, all 60 samples with different proportions can be completely separated from each other, resulting in 100% accuracy. The six unknown samples containing binary samples were analyzed, and the classification accuracy was 100% (Table S4), and the corresponding curves displaying the relationship between the true and predicted categories and the R2 of the linear fitting curve was 1 (Fig. 4d). The concentration accuracy of the samples was also 100% (Table S5). With high prediction accuracy, the results further demonstrate that our sensor array can simultaneously and successfully detect different binary mixture samples and better deal with complex situations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distinguishing metal ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: We further validated the discriminative ability of the sensor array in real samples. The 288 real samples were prepared by spiking different concentrations (0.5–10 μM) of four single metal ions into the prepared soil samples and lake water (Figure S16 and S17). As shown in Fig. 5 a and Figure S18, the entire sample was correctly identified using the SX-model with 100% and 93.3% accuracy in soil samples and lake water, respectively. Next, 30 unknown samples (soil and lake water) were selected to test the classification and concentration accuracy of the model. The corresponding curves displaying the relationship between the true category and predicted category and the R2 of the linear fitting curve was 1.0 and 0.94 in soil samples and lake water, respectively (Figure S19 and Figure S20). In addition, the concentration accuracy of unknown samples was 100% in the soil and lake water (Table S6 and Table S7). The above results show the excellent feasibility of the sensor array for discriminating unknown samples.

Similarly, 60 multidimensional data were selected from binary mixtures of Fe2+/Cr6+, Cu2+/Cr6+ and Fe2+/Fe3+ in soil (Figure S21), and a 100% classification accuracy was obtained, as shown in Fig. 5b. Then, all unknown samples were correctly identified with 100% classification and concentration accuracy, as shown in Table S8 and Table S9. These results are similar to those obtained from ICP − MS (Table S10) which demonstrates the high accuracy and reliability of the proposed method for quantitative determination of multiple heavy metal ions in real samples.

Traditional analytical methods build separate models at each concentration, and it is nearly impossible to bring unknown samples into suitable models and limit their application in practical situations. We built an original generalized model and tested its performance in deionized water and real samples. Then, 75 unknown samples with various concentrations were identified using the SX-model, with an average classification accuracy of 96% (Table S2, Table S11 and Table S12). The mean concentration accuracy of unknown samples reached 100% in deionized water, soil samples and lake water (Table S3, Table S6 and Table S7).

In conventional array detection studies, different binary mixed samples were identified simultaneously. To examine the performance of our SX-model in dealing with complex environments, an accuracy of 100% was achieved in deionized water and soil samples (Fig. 4c and Fig. 5b).

Based on the above results, it is determined that the SX model can provide accurate identifications in a variety of complex environments. Compared with traditional methods, the SX-model has effectively broken the bottleneck in practical applications. Moreover, because the SX-model is established under the framework of TPOT, it can be automatically adjusted into the best model based on data input, instead of operating as a fixed model. Therefore, as an optimal recognition model, the SX-model is universal and has the potential to assist in array detection research. This promotes the development of array detection and analysis methods.

A quantitative detection method for heavy metals in complex samples was constructed by adopting a “stepwise prediction” strategy combined with machine-learning in an innovative manner. In addition, a multi-emission array sensor mode was proposed to obtain multidimensional data through simple methods. The seven heavy metals were distinguished using a multi-emission fluorescent sensor array based on carbon dots and EDTA-Tb3+ lanthanide, with the TPOT framework found to be the most accurate method, successfully identifying seven heavy metals at concentrations as low as 0.05 μM. Coupled with the sensor and SX-model, different concentrations of metal ions could be correctly discriminated from real samples. In addition, such a multi-emission fluorescent array showed high sensitivity, adequate anti-interference, and the ability to detect heavy metals in soil and lake water media. More importantly, the SX-model was originally employed and evaluated in the field of array sensing detection, showing 100% accuracy in the concentration detection of unknown samples. This study demonstrates the great potential of a multi-emission fluorescent sensor array for the rapid, efficient, and low detection limit of multiple heavy metals in complex environments. In addition, the original establishment of the SX-model with universality assists array detection and meets the challenges of establishing a universal model for array detection.
Funding Sources.
Key Technologies Research and Development Program of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2017B03017-5).
Key Technologies Research and Development Program of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2017B03017-3).
Key Laboratory construction project of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2017D03025).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the program of Key Technologies Research and Development Program of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2017B03017-5), Key Technologies Research and Development Program of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2017B03017-3) and Key Laboratory construction project of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2017D03025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.135690.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Skoulikidis, Heavy metal contamination status in Greek surface waters: A review with application and evaluation of pollution indices
